Thousands of mice swarm parts of Western Australia in a ‘mouse plague’

(date: 2026-06-13)

SYDNEY — The mice are everywhere. Each morning, residents of Morawa, a remote town in Western Australia, check for carcasses in the traps they set at bedtime. They inspect their kettles for little bodies before making tea. They wipe down kitchen benches in case rodents visited at night. Then they head to work and do it all again.

Navy Wants Sailors to Do Less Supplemental Work on USS Harry S. Truman Overhaul

(date: 2026-06-12)

The Navy is turning to commercial contractors for more duties during the upcoming refueling and complex overhaul of USS Harry S. Truman (CVN-75) in order to free up more time for sailors, USNI News has learned. Previously, sailors assigned to a ship in RCOH would be assigned supplementary, non-rate specific duties, such as painting, insulating or transportation services. Now, those duties will be given to contractors, and sailors will be able to use the time for training and operational readiness, reads a Navy release given to USNI News. Sailors will still perform rate-specific duties and, if necessary, could perform non-rate

Mieko Fujimoto, 92, of Paauilo died May 24 at home. Born in Kukuihaele, she was a retired secretary for Paauilo School and office manager for Fujimoto Super Service. Visitation 9-10 a.m. Thursday (June 18) at Dodo Mortuary Chapel. Funeral service at 10 a.m. Private burial to follow at East Hawaii Veterans Cemetery No. 2. Casual attire. Survived by daughter, Jasmine Bailado of Paauilo; sister, Setsuko (James) Kawamura of California; sister-in-law, Julia Kusumoto of Oahu; two grandsons and four great-grandchildren; nieces and nephews. Arrangements by Dodo Mortuary.

Pricey World Cup keeps fans away, hits US hotels, airlines

(date: 2026-06-12, updated: 2026-06-13)

NEW YORK — Hours before the World Cup kickoff, the boost to travel and tourism expected from this year’s biggest sporting event has yet to materialize. For years, the tournament was expected to deliver a windfall for America’s travel industry, now grappling with declining international visitors amid what rights groups describe as a climate of fear. The swarms of fans, hotels had counted on have yet to arrive, forcing many to cut rates. Flight bookings have slumped as ticket prices have skyrocketed. Expensive match tickets have further stymied demand, and industry analysts say excitement has been muted compared with past World Cups.

2026 NVTHS Graduation — “Make A Mark and Success Stories”

(date: 2026-06-11)

By

Samoa News staff

reporters@samoanews.com

Pago Pago, AMERICAN SAMOA — Receiving congratulations and their diploma was an integral part of the Nuuuli Vocational Technical High School Commencement Ceremony with Principal Susana Kaisa as the Master of Ceremonies.

The invocation was offered by Rev. Malieitulua, who quoted Proverbs 3:16 “Commit to the Lord whatever you do and He will establish your plans.” He told the 75 graduates to “Trust in the Lord…”

Gov. Pulaalii presented special remarks stating, “Before you make your mark on this world, remember God has already placed his mark on you…” He continued with words of comfort to the graduates saying, “He knows your name, he knows your work, and he knows your unique contribution only you can make… Trust that God will walk beside you… The world needs people who know how to build, create, decide, serve and solve problems… these are just not jobs, but skills that strengthen families, businesses and communities… every project accomplished, every decision made, every person helped… these leave your marks.”

Keynote speaker ASTCA director Fata, a NVTHS alumni, noted her return to her high school after 26 years. She characterized herself as a troublemaker while a NVTHS student, and didn’t remember anything about her graduation other than hoping it would hurry up so she could go celebrate. She briefly described her journey from that moment to now, and asked the graduates to remember whatever your bad decisions, you are the one to make it better… and to remember without love, without understanding, the things you do are nothing. Fata emphasized that without God nothing is accomplished. To parents, Fata asked that they do not compare their child to another because each one is unique.

NVTHS 2026 GRADUATES NUMBER 75[[photo: AF]

The 75 Nuuuli Vocational Technical High School Wildcat graduates after receiving their diplomas during the Commencement Ceremony held this week, June 8, 2026 at the school’s gymnasium.

Top students announced during the ceremony were Valedictorian — Ms Tumua Sauvai Faitau (front row- right wearing green sash), and Salutatorian — Mr. Von Javien Samoa Siona (far left front row wearing sunglasses)

Gifting of graduation paraphernalia after a graduation commencement ceremony is the highlight of many graduates, where they are recognized by their family and friends for a milestone accomplished in their lives. [photo: AF]

Section:

Local News

Report to Congress on U.S.-South Korea Alliance

(date: 2026-06-11, updated: 2026-06-12)

The following is the June 9, 2026, Congressional Research Service report, The U.S.-South Korea Alliance. From the Report The U.S.-South Korea (Republic of Korea, or ROK) alliance dates to the 1950-1953 Korean War, during which more than 36,000 Americans died in-theater helping South Korea repel an invasion by North Korea (Democratic People’s Republic of Korea, or DPRK) intended to reunify the country under communist rule. An Armistice Agreement was signed in 1953, the same year the United States and South Korea signed the U.S.-ROK Mutual Defense Treaty, which commits the United States to defend South Korea, particularly from North Korea.

Obituaries for June 11

(date: 2026-06-11, updated: 2026-06-12)

Francis Walter “Frank” Wright Carney, 59, of Ocean View died May 20 on the Island of Hawaii. Born in Winchester, Mass., he grew up in New York City where he attended Seward Park High School and was an owner/operator of Frank’s Marie and Precision Auto in Florida.. Memorial service 6:30 p.m. Friday, June 19, at His Presence Ministries, 15-1649 12th Ave. in Hawaiian Paradise Park, Keaau. No flowers, memorial donations can be made to Queen Esther’s Hawaii. Survived by wife, Mei Carney of Ocean View; sons, Timothy Wright, Steven Wright, and Noah Wright Carney; stepson, Kiel Higgins; mother, Judy (Joseph) Carrozo; sisters, Francine Schill and Paulette Wright; brother, Paul Carney; three grandchildren. Arrangements by Dodo Mortuary.

Taiwanese Forces Deploy American-made Attack Drones Against Maritime Targets

(date: 2026-06-10, updated: 2026-06-12)

Taiwan’s newly acquired American attack drones struck maritime targets for the first time last week along the island’s west coast during live-fire drills designed to validate long-range strike systems that would play a crucial role in a potential invasion of the self-governing island. According to Taiwanese military media, the drones successfully struck their offshore targets following a simulated engagement loop where troops rehearsed detecting and identifying the threat. At least three towed flatbed launchers equipped with four Altius drones each were deployed for the exercise. The Republic of China Army received around 2,000 Altius-600 loitering munitions from American defense firm

BALTOPS 2026 Exercise Begins

(date: 2026-06-10, updated: 2026-06-12)

NATO kicked off maritime exercise Baltic Operations (BALTOPS) 2026 this week with 20 ships and 6,000 personnel from 15 NATO nations – fewer than BALTOPS 2025 owing to the NATO naval assets wrapped up in the Middle East. U.S. Sixth Fleet, which leads the exercise, said in a press release that, for the first time since 1972, the exercise will be command-and-controlled from Allied Joint Force Command (JFC) Brunssum to realistically imitate NATO command structure. “BALTOPS forms for us three vital tasks: deters Russian threats in the Baltic Sea region, builds real world readiness and interoperability, strengthen alliance’s cohesion” said

(date: 2026-06-10, updated: 2026-06-12)

The two sailors involved in a fatal shooting Saturday aboard future aircraft carrier John F. Kennedy (CVN-79) were part of the ship’s armed security forces, USNI News has learned. Boatswain’s Mate Seaman Jesse Braswell, of Colorado, was killed in the shooting, which took place around 5:15 a.m on Saturday, while another unnamed sailor was taken into pre-trial confinement. The two were part of the in-port security force for the carrier while the ship is being completed at HII’s Newport News Shipbuilding, a defense official told USNI News. Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints encouraging a special fast

(date: 2026-06-10)

By

Samoa News staff

reporters@samoanews.com

Pagp Pago, AMERICAN SAMOA — On July 5, 2026, the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints is encouraging its American members to participate in a special fast. For Latter-day Saints, the 250th anniversary commemorations are not merely a historic milestone for the country, but an opportunity to reflect on their faith’s relationship to the American experiment.

Of note, on Monday this week, the Pentagon updated its ‘revised religious affiliation list’ that it announced last week Friday — which didn’t list the LDS church under a subcategory for “Christian” denominations, after outrage from LDS lawmakers. The revamped list eliminated the Christian subcategory completely and instead listed denominations without the Christian moniker.

One of the LDS lawmakers, in a video post before the revamped version was announced by the Pentagon, stated that he found it “repugnant to any sense of decency, any sense of our common heritage and our common belief that the government needs to not weigh in on doctrinal disputes between various religious denominations.”

The Pentagon, on Monday, agreed saying, “the Pentagon’s job is not to adjudicate theological debates, but instead to ensure sincerely-held faith is respected and encouraged in our ranks.”

Section:

Local News

Tags:

Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints

https://www.samoanews.com/local-news/church-jesus-christ-latter-day-saints-encouraging-special-fast-july-5th

Air Force Recruitment Team on island

(date: 2026-06-10)

Pago Pago, AMERICAN SAMOA — The Air Force Recruitment Team paid a courtesy visit to the Governor’s Office shortly before graduation season, where they met with Lt. Governor Pulumataala Ae Ae Jr. to discuss opportunities for engaging and recruiting local youth into the Air Force.

The team shared that they currently do not have an established office within the Territory and expressed their interest in working collaboratively with the American Samoa Government to establish an official recruiting presence locally.

Lt. Governor Pulumataala acknowledged the Air Force Recruitment Team’s efforts and welcomed continued collaboration to expand opportunities and awareness for the youth of the Territory.

Local News

USAF RECRUITERS

Obituaries for June 10

(date: 2026-06-10)

Katherine “Mieke” Inouye, 72, of Papaikou died April 11 at her son Benjamin’s home in Monrovia, Calif. Born in Topeka, Kan., she was a breeder of border collies and member of the Daughters of the American Revolution. Visitation 10-11 Friday, June 19, at Church of the Holy Cross, 440 W. Lanikaula St. in Hilo. Celebration of life service at 11 a.m. Refreshments after the service. No flowers; memorial donations to Aloha ‘Ilio Rescue in Keaau. Survived by husband, Gordon Inouye; sons, Benjamin (Kimberly) Inouye and Aaron (Larae) Inouye; siblings, Erick (Holly) Bickett, Jill (Barry) Cordsen and Liesbet (Hans) Bickett; four grandchildren; cousins, nieces, nephews, grandnieces, grandnephews, aunts and uncles. Arrangements by Douglass and Zook Funeral and Cremation Services in Monrovia, Calif. and Dodo Mortuary.

WestPac to discuss Albacore Access, Seabed Mining at Am Samoa Meeting

(date: 2026-06-09)

HONOLULU — The Western Pacific Regional Fishery Management Council will hold its 207th meeting June 9–11, 2026, at the Tauese P.F. Sunia Ocean Center in American Samoa to discuss major fishery issues affecting the territory and the wider Pacific.

A key focus will be South Pacific albacore tuna, an important fishery for American Samoa’s longline fleet, cannery and supporting businesses. The Council will review tuna allocation scenarios and criteria being developed through the Western and Central Pacific Fisheries Commission, where international negotiations are underway. For American Samoa, the discussion centers on ensuring the territory’s fleet has meaningful access under any future regional catch limits. This access could help restore South Pacific albacore as a viable fishery for one of the last truly island-based albacore operations in the Pacific.

“The Council is working to make sure American Samoa receives a fair share of any South Pacific albacore allocation to revive our local longline fleet,” said Council Chair Nathan Ilaoa. “South Pacific albacore and the tuna industry do more than support fishing jobs. They help lower the cost of fuel, electricity and shipping for our islands, while strengthening food security and sustaining a Pacific Island fishery that operates under strong U.S. standards.”

The Council will also receive updates from the BOEM and NOAA’s National Ocean Service on proposed deep-sea mineral mining near American Samoa and in the Clarion-Clipperton Zone, an area of the high seas outside the U.S. exclusive economic zone. Federal agencies are reviewing mining interest in the Pacific, while Council advisors, community members and local leaders have raised concerns about potential impacts to fisheries, ocean ecosystems and future generations. The Council may provide comments or recommendations on deep-sea mining activities in the Western Pacific.

Other agenda items include outcomes from the first Bigeye Tuna Management Procedure Workshop, held in May, and an update on electronic monitoring implementation for the Hawai‘i and American Samoa longline fisheries. Electronic monitoring is intended to support fishery data collection and improve coverage while vessels are at sea. The Council will also consider final action on annual catch limit specifications for the main Hawaiian Islands Kona crab fishery for 2027–2030.

As part of Council meeting week, the public is invited to attend the Fishers Forum, “Fishing for Science,” Tuesday, June 9, from 6 to 8:30 p.m. at the Tauese P.F. Sunia Ocean Center in Utulei. The free, family friendly event will show how fisheries data supports management decisions and the future of fishing in American Samoa. The evening will include informational tables, presentations and recognition for participants.

The 207th Council meeting is open to the public, with public comment opportunities scheduled during the meeting and remote participation available by Webex.

How to Join

In-Person: Tauese P.F. Sunia Ocean Center, Utulei, AS

Meeting Dates: June 9-11, 2026

Fishers Forum: Tuesday, June 9, 6-8:30 p.m.

Online: Via Webex: https://tinyurl.com/207CouncilMtg, Event password: CM207mtg

Section:

Local News

Tags:

Western Pacific Regional Fishery Management Council

https://www.samoanews.com/local-news/westpac-discuss-albacore-access-seabed-mining-american-samoa-meeting

(date: 2026-06-09, updated: 2026-06-12)

The following is the June 4, 2026, Congressional Research Service report, Taiwan: Defense and Military Issues. From the report Taiwan, (which formally calls itself the Republic of China, or ROC) is a self-governing democracy of 23.3 million people located across the Taiwan Strait from mainland China. The People’s Republic of China (PRC, or China) claims but has never controlled Taiwan. PRC leaders have stated their preference to unify peacefully with Taiwan, but have insisted on the right to use force to bring Taiwan under PRC control. Obituaries for June 9

(date: 2026-06-09)

John Joseph Hegarty, 84, of Hilo, died April 18 at Yukio Okutsu State Veterans Home. Born in Ireland, he was a retired public works superintendent at Schofield Barracks Army installation on Oahu and U.S. Army veteran. Visitation noon-2 p.m. Wednesday (June 10) at Dodo Mortuary Chapel. Memorial services at 2 p.m. Casual attire; no flowers. Survived by wife, Janet Hegarty of Hilo; son, Donovan (Tanya) Hegarty of Waimea; stepdaughter, Donna (Nolan) Empron of Keaau; stepson, William “Billy” (Sharon) Lee of Waipahu, Oahu; brother, Michael Hegarty of England; eight grandchildren and 13 great-grandchildren; nieces and nephews. Arrangements by Dodo Mortuary.

SEOUL/BEIJING — China will not swerve from its commitment to safeguarding common interests with North Korea or waver in its support for Kim Jong Un, President Xi Jinping told the North’s leader on Monday during a rare Pyongyang summit. (date: 2026-06-08, updated: 2026-06-12)

China deployed six cutters and patrol ships east of the Luzon Strait in its second “special maritime law enforcement operation” following a diplomatic row with the Philippines and Japan. On Saturday, China’s Ministry of Transport led a mission involving the Fujian and Guangdong regional maritime security administrations east of Taiwan. Chinese state media claims that the operation was carried out to protest recent talks between the Philippines and Japan. (date: 2026-06-08, updated: 2026-06-12)

Aircraft carrier USS Theodore Roosevelt (CVN-71) will lead the U.S. participation in this year’s Rim of the Pacific (RIMPAC) 2026 exercise, the service announced. Held from June 24 to July 31 in and around the Hawaiian Islands, RIMPAC will include a total of 32 surface ships, five submarines with 31 nations participating in the drills. Theodore Roosevelt will lead a U.S. contingent comprising of cruiser USS Chosin (CG-65), destroyers USS Paul Hamilton (DDG-60), USS Decatur (DDG-73), USS Wayne E. Meyer (DDG-108) and USS Carl M. US Supreme Court poised to rule on gun laws and transgender athletes

(date: 2026-06-08)

WASHINGTON — Disputes involving President Donald Trump’s policies on tariffs, immigration and firing regulatory officials have drawn a lot of attention during the Supreme Court’s current term, but the justices also are poised to decide major cases concerning U.S. “culture wars” issues such as guns and transgender athletes. The court is expected to wrap up its nine-month term by around the end of this month. In two major cases, it is due to rule in challenges to a U.S. law barring users of illegal drugs from owning guns and a Hawaii law that restricts the carrying of handguns on private property open to the public, like most businesses, without the owner’s permission. It also is set to decide the legality of laws in Idaho and West Virginia banning transgender athletes from female sports teams amid intensifying efforts by the Republican president and various states to restrict the rights of transgender people.

The post US Supreme Court poised to rule on gun laws and transgender athletes appeared first on Hawaii Tribune-Herald.

Lawmakers began voting on amendments to the immigration bill in a “vote-a-rama” session that was expected to end in a vote on the underlying measure later in the day. Obituaries for June 5

(date: 2026-06-05)

Jovy Muriel AhLinLoy Kawehionapua Hauanio, 33, of Pahoa died April 25, at Hilo Benioff Medical Center. Born in Hilo, she worked for Pahoa No. 1 Chinese restaurant, Suisan, Sustainable Propane and Malama Market, and was a member of Over Coming Faith and Hilo Queens Wahine Flag Football. Visitation 9-10 a.m. Friday, June 12, at Dodo Mortuary Chapel. Funeral service at 10 a.m. Cremation to follow. Aloha attire. Survived by partner/significant other, Mana Brooks; children, Jubilee Hauanio, Rylee Brooks, Ryder Brooks, Ryden Brooks and Ryiot Brooks; mother, Shawn Aiona; hanai mother, Judy Aiona; maternal grandmother, Sophie Aiona; brothers, John-Ben “JB” (Casey-Leigh) Hauanio, Haili (Tita K) Puerto and Kawika Haili; hanai brother, Jorden “Ikaika” (Kapua) Aiona-Waipa; hanai sisters, Jade (Tyson) Aiona and Gerlyn (Louie) Solis; aunts, uncles, nieces, nephews and cousins. Arrangements by Dodo Mortuary.

Sandra King Young officially registers campaign committee with the Federal Election Commission

(date: 2026-06-04)

Based On A SKY Press Release

Pago Pago, AMERICAN SAMOA — Sandra King Young announced that she has officially registered her campaign committee with the Federal Election Commission (FEC), marking the official launch of her campaign for Delegate to the U.S. House of Representatives from American Samoa.

The filing formally establishes the campaign under federal law and authorizes the committee to raise funds, report financial activity, and operate in full compliance with federal election requirements.

"Registering with the Federal Election Commission is more than a legal requirement — it is a commitment to transparency, accountability, and public trust," said King Young. "It is also the first step in building a campaign focused on the future of American Samoa and the challenges facing our people in Washington, D.C."

The Federal Election Commission is the independent agency responsible for enforcing federal campaign finance laws. Registration ensures public disclosure of campaign contributions and expenditures, providing voters with transparency into how campaigns are funded and operated.

King Young said her decision to run is rooted in part by a simple belief: American Samoa needs proactive representation that identifies challenges early and acts before they become crises.

"The cost of inaction is simply too high," she said. "Today, we face critical issues that will shape the future of our territory for generations. The growing threat of deep-sea mining demands immediate action to ensure responsible stewardship of our ocean resources at the same time, pursuing more responsible economic development.

“The omission of American Samoa's permanent residents from Medicare eligibility under the One Big Beautiful Act threatens healthcare access for many families. And the dismantling of the U.S. Department of Education raises serious concerns about the future of federal support for our public and private schools.

“These are just a few of the many critical issues that I will champion if elected as your representative to Congress. These are not just tomorrow's issues—they are today's issues, and they require proactive leadership now.”

King Young added, "This campaign is dedicated to protecting our interests, expanding economic opportunity, strengthening healthcare, supporting education, and ensuring that American Samoa has a proactive, strong, and respected voice in Congress."

Over the coming months, King Young will engage voters across the territory through village visits, community meetings, and direct conversations about the issues that matter most to American Samoa's future.

For more information or to receive campaign updates, please call 252-VOTE, 770-VOTE, or email vote@sandrakingyoung.com.

Section:

Local News

Tags:

Sandra King Young

https://www.samoanews.com/local-news/sandra-king-young-officially-registers-campaign-committee-federal-election-commission

Local student selected to explore the Ocean aboard E/V Nautilus

(date: 2026-06-04)

Pago Pago, AMERICAN SAMOA — Jhen Allison Paguiligan Seguiwan, a student at Southern Utah University, has been selected as a 2026 Seafloor Mapping & Hydrography Intern in the Science & Engineering Internship Program and will sail aboard Ocean Exploration Trust (OET)’s Exploration Vessel (E/V) Nautilus during its 2026 expedition season.

Born and raised in American Samoa and an alumna of Faasao Marist High School, Jhen was selected from hundreds of applicants as one of 19 interns and will join the team aboard E/V Nautilus in June for 13 days at sea to map never-before-seen seafloor between Hawaiʻi and Guam. Not only will this expedition be the first for the new acoustic multibeam mapping system on E/V Nautilus, but it will also include open-ocean biodiversity and seabird surveys from the ship.

“Growing up in American Samoa, what many would call just a ‘dot on the map’, taught me that opportunities do not always come to you,” says Jhen. “You have to be curious enough to seek them out. It is an honor to represent my home through this expedition, and I hope my journey inspires future generations to dream big, embrace new opportunities, and realize that no goal is out of reach!”

Ocean Exploration Trust, a nonprofit founded by Dr. Robert Ballard, operates with a mission to explore the unknown parts of the ocean, seeking out new discoveries while pushing the boundaries of technological innovation, STEAM education, and outreach. Expeditions center on scientific exploration of the seafloor, collaboration with the broader scientific community, and engagement of audiences of all ages via telepresence technology. The team has traveled around the world, helping understand the deep ocean and sharing discoveries live with the world through NautilusLive.org.

As a Seafloor Mapping & Hydrography Engineering Intern, Jhen will learn to acquire, manage, and process seafloor mapping data from a deep-ocean multibeam sonar, helping to close gaps in high-resolution data coverage worldwide.

OET’s internships train students in the at-sea environment and provide real-world experience to further develop academic interests and professional career paths via participation in the Nautilus Exploration Program. These interns come from community colleges, undergraduate, service academies, and graduate programs in ocean and atmospheric sciences, seafloor mapping, systems engineering, film, and electrical, mechanical, aerospace, and robotics engineering.

People worldwide can engage with Jhen and the explorers onboard via NautilusLive.org, a 24-hour live-streaming web portal that brings expeditions from the field to explorers on shore through telepresence technology and social media. Local schools, summer programs, and community groups are encouraged to schedule free, live one-on-one Q&A sessions with explorers on the ship available daily.

About the Ocean Exploration Trust

The Ocean Exploration Trust was founded in 2007 by Dr. Robert Ballard to explore the ocean, seeking out multi-disciplinary discoveries while pushing the boundaries of STEAM education and technological innovation. Our international program is launched from aboard the Exploration Vessel (E/V) Nautilus, offering live exploration to participants on shore and the public via live video, audio, and data feeds. Learn more about Ocean Exploration Trust.

The 2026 E/V Nautilus expeditions are sponsored by NOAA Ocean Exploration via the Ocean Exploration Cooperative Institute, including additional support from the Bureau of Ocean Energy Management. OET program sponsors and partners for 2026 also include LeeWay Marine, the Office of Naval Research, Flying Fish, National Center for Education & the Economy, QPS, and the Phillip Stephenson Foundation. Learn more about our partners and sponsors.

Section:

Local News

E/V Nautilus

The Philippines is set to receive transfers of American and Japanese vessels to bolster its Coast Guard and Navy fleets operating in the South China Sea. American Samoa’s July 4th celebration will be “one for the books”

(date: 2026-06-03)

Women’s Cricket, Ruby Sevens, Live Concert, Fautasi Race, Fireworks & Drone Show

Source: ASTCA press release

Pago Pago, AMERICAN SAMOA — A full slate of major community events and tournaments are on tap to mark America’s 250th Independence Day on July 4th. Organizers say this year’s festivities will be “one for the books,” bringing families, athletes, and music lovers from across the islands.

Reid Group (GHC Reid & Company, Samoa Motors, Pago Motors, Vaimalu Puried Water, and Peter E. Reid Stevedoring) has joined forces with ASTCA and ASG, as key partners for the Independence Day events.

The celebration will feature:

• Women’s Cricket Tournament

• The iconic Fautasi Race

• An International 7s Rugby Tournament

• A live concert headlined by Stan Walker, with performances by Kennyon Brown and DJ Noiz.

• Ending it all with a FIREWORK AND DRONE SHOW

WOMEN’S CRICKET TOURNAMENT

This past Saturday, May 30th, the 250th Women’s Cricket Tournament was launched in Vailoa. The ceremonial first ball was delivered by Niko Wendt, CEO of Reid Group, signifying the commencement of the tournament. The 250th Women’s Cricket Teams are only from the villages and consist of 19 teams;

1. Pago Girls of Pago Pago

2. Teine ole I’a of Fagasa

3. Teine ole Aga of Auto & Amaua

4. Teine ole Malaeoletalu of Fagatogo

5. Teine ole Fetulima of Atu’u

6. Teine ole Tasiaeafe of Afono

7. Teine ole Asaga of Aunu’u

8. Teine ole Manuula of Vatia

9. Teine ole Seaula of Aoa

10. Teine ole Muaau of Faleniu

11. Teine ole Pisisami of Vaitogi

12. Teine ole Fa’asaulala of Vailoa

13. Teine ole Fale o Latia of Pavaiai

14. Lady Bird of Nu’uuli

15. Teine ole Lupelele of Iliili

16. Mosooipala of Leone

17. Teine ole Taputapu of Amanave

18. Teine ole Fetu o le Afiafi Faga'alu

19. Teine ole Mana of Nua & Seetaga

“We have announced the prizes at three different meetings with all the team captains and team managers, increasing the amount each time,” said 250th Women’s Cricket Chairman, Director Mariana Timu-Faiai.

“At the kickoff, we set the first prize at $18,000, the second prize at $15,000, the third prize at $13,000, and the fourth prize at $10,000. All other participating teams will each receive $7,000.”

“We are deeply grateful to Reid Group and the American Samoa Government for partnering with ASTCA to bring this massive celebration to our community. Their unwavering support and collaboration have made it possible to create an event of this scale and significance.

“On behalf of ASTCA, I extend my heartfelt thanks to everyone involved — together, we are strengthening our community and creating lasting memories for all.” said ASTCA CEO Mrs. Folasaitu Sorepa Fata Thomas.

ASTCA organizers invite everyone in the community to join them and their valued partners to celebrate this historic milestone — the 250th Independence Day — stating that it promises to be one of the most memorable in American Samoa’s history.

Local News

French Navy Seizes Sanctioned Russian Tanker, 4th Since September

(date: 2026-06-03, updated: 2026-06-11)

The French Navy seized sanctioned tanker MT Tagor on Sunday while it operated in the Atlantic, marking the service’s fourth shadow fleet seizure since September. The tanker was seized on suspicion of flying a false Cameroonian flag and diverted to France, arriving Tuesday in Douarnenez Bay where the ship is detained, the Maritime Prefecture of the Atlantic announced Monday. Headed by a naval officer, the Maritime Prefect is France’s representative at sea, responsible for maritime safety. The seizure occurred 400 nautical miles west of the tip of the French peninsula of Brittany. Tagor reportedly traveled from Murmansk, Russia, according to

https://news.usni.org/2026/06/03/french-navy-seizes-sanctioned-russian-tanker-4th-since-september

Trump appoints ally Bill Pulte as acting US intelligence director

(date: 2026-06-03, updated: 2026-06-04)

President Donald Trump appointed federal housing regulator Bill Pulte as acting director of national intelligence on Tuesday, elevating a political loyalist with no national security experience to lead the sprawling U.S. intelligence community at a time of war and global tensions. Pulte, 38, has used his position as head of a low-profile mortgage regulatory agency to push for investigations of several of Trump’s perceived enemies for alleged mortgage fraud. None have yet resulted in criminal charges. Pulte replaces the departing Tulsi Gabbard in the intelligence post.

A "Super" El Nino is building in the tropical Pacific Ocean

(date: 2026-06-02)

Compiled by Samoa News staff

Pago Pago, AMERICAN SAMOA — Climatologists from NOAA’s Climate Prediction Center forecast are predicting an 82% chance an El Niño pattern will develop between May and July, with the probability of El Niño conditions persisting through early 2027 rising above 96%.

El Niño conditions occur when abnormally warm waters build in tropical region of the central and eastern Pacific Ocean and are usually associated with a weakening of the easterly trade winds, sometimes even reversing to westerlies. Consequently, tropical rains that usually fall over Indonesia move eastward; sea level decreases in the western Pacific; and the vertical, thermal structure of the ocean and coastal and upwelling currents are changed.

EL NIÑO AND ITS IMPACTS ON AMERICAN SAMOA

Rainfall during the year of a strong El Niño starts out below normal in August and dryness continues through June of the year after El Niño. These long -term dry conditions may be punctuated with intermittent wet conditions. The level of these dry conditions depends on the intensity of the El Niño event. For strong El Niño’s, rainfall begins to return to normal by August of the year following that strong El Niño event. With weak and moderate El Niño’s rainfall can be above normal, however this response is not consistent.

During strong El Niño’s, the tropical cyclone season exhibits an enhanced risk of a damaging tropical cyclone from November through January. The risk falls away to near or below average from February through April as TC activity pushes farther to the east. These features of the tropical cyclone season are less distinct during weak El Niño’s.

Sea level in American Samoa usually starts falling during October or November of the El Niño year. The fall is 3-6 months later, when compared to North Pacific islands. Sea levels are lower than average for the entire year after El Niño. Taimasa is the local term given to these conditions.

During El Niño, ocean waters tend to warm substantially in the central and eastern equatorial Pacific at both the surface and subsurface. In American Samoa, however, near or even slightly cooler than normal ocean waters are commonly observed during strong El Nino’s.

EVERY EL NIÑO IS A LITTLE BIT DIFFERENT

El Niño conditions can start to develop as early as May or June and typically reaches maximum strength during December; the conditions then subside towards normal conditions by June of the following year. However, the evolution and duration, strength and impacts of individual El Niño events can vary, in some cases greatly. This makes constant monitoring and awareness extremely important for decision makers across multiple sectors.

BACKGROUND

The El Niño – Southern Oscillation is a recurring climate pattern involving changes in the temperature of waters in the central and eastern tropical Pacific Ocean and the patterns of sea level pressure, lower- and upper-level winds, and tropical rainfall across the Pacific basin. On periods ranging from about two to seven years, the surface waters across a large swath of the tropical Pacific Ocean warm or cool by anywhere from 1°C to 3°C, compared to normal. This irregular oscillation between warm and cool patterns, referred to as the ENSO cycle, directly affects rainfall distribution in the tropics and can have a strong influence on weather across the Pacific basin.

(Sources: NOAA, Agricultural Weather Forum)

Chinese Carrier Strike Group Conducts 170 Sorties During Drills in Philippine Sea

(date: 2026-06-02, updated: 2026-06-11)

The People’s Liberation Army Navy (PLAN) Liaoning Carrier Strike Group has conducted 170 sorties in the past week as the group trains in the Philippine Sea east of Luzon while shadowed by the Japan Maritime Self-Defense Force. Embarked fighter aircraft and helicopters were observed doing 80 take offs and landings May 26, 50 on Wednesday and 40 on Thursday, according to Japan’s Joint Staff Office. Japanese destroyer JS Asahi (DD-119) shadowed the Chinese carrier strike group, which includes carrier CNS Liaoning (16), cruiser CNS Wuxi (104), frigate CNS Luohe (545), destroyer CNS Kaifeng (124) and fast combat support ship CNS

(date: 2026-06-02, updated: 2026-06-10)

The following is the May 26, 2026, Congressional Research Service report, U.S. Foreign Policy in the Western Hemisphere: Issues for Congress. From the Report Summary Historically, many U.S. policymakers have regarded the Western Hemisphere as a U.S. sphere of influence, vital to U.S. interests, or both. U.S. engagement in the region has shifted over time, responding to changes in the hemisphere and in U.S. objectives. Following the end of the Cold War, the U.S. approach to the Western Hemisphere primarily sought to promote democracy and human rights, reduce barriers to trade, and combat transnational security threats. David “Dave” Kaleookalani Awai, 51, of Hilo died April 8 at Hilo Benioff Medical Center. Born in Hilo, he was a foreman for Awai’s Masonry and foreman and partner for Awai and Sons Concrete. Visitation 9-10 a.m. Saturday, June 13, at Dodo Mortuary Chapel. Funeral service at 10 a.m. Cremation to follow. Aloha attire. Survived by fiancée, Debi Vierra of Hilo; children, Daven (Talisah Tabango) Awai of Orem, Utah, Lexani Awai and Denali Awai of Hilo; stepchildren, Kiara “Kiki” Reyes of Kona, Lopaka Diego, Lanakila Diego and Lokelani Diego of Hilo; mother, Isabelle Awai of Hilo; siblings, Rochelle (Eugene) Travis of Henderson, Nev. and Douglas (Cheryl) Awai, Jr. of Hilo; a step-grandson; aunts, uncles, nieces, nephews and cousins. Arrangements by Dodo Mortuary.

(date: 2026-06-01, updated: 2026-06-10)

At least 200 people have been killed in U.S. strikes on suspected drug boats after forces with Joint Task Force – Operation Southern Spear hit two boats over the weekend. The U.S. struck two boats in the Eastern Pacific – one on Friday and one on Saturday – killing three men in each strike. The six deaths bring Operation Southern Spear’s total death toll to at least 200. There are at least five survivors whose status are unknown but are likely presumed dead. Territorial Administration on Aging celebrates Senior Citizens Day

(date: 2026-06-01)

Source: ASG press release

INDUSTRIAL PARK TAFUNA, American Samoa — Governor Pulaali’i Nikolao Pula joined Congresswoman Aumua Amata, distinguished guests, and community leaders at the Territorial Administration on Aging (TAOA) to celebrate Senior Citizens Day recently, honoring the treasured elders of American Samoa.

The ceremony opened under the leadership of TAOA Deputy Director Faau Seumanutafa, who welcomed attendees with warm appreciation. Rev. Fetaiai Fui, also Deputy Director for TAOA, offered the invocation and spiritual message, followed by a beautiful hymn presented by the TAOA senior citizens.

Governor Pulaali’i delivered heartfelt remarks recognizing the lifelong contributions of American Samoa’s senior citizens. He spoke on the strength, wisdom, and service of the elder community, emphasizing that their longevity is a blessing from God. The Governor encouraged continued focus on health and well‐being, noting that the knowledge and love shared by the senior community remain invaluable gifts to the territory.

The program continued with special remarks by Congresswoman Aumua Amata, who highlighted that the longevity of our seniors is a reflection of their prayers and faith in God. She reaffirmed her commitment to working closely with TAOA to expand benefits and support services for senior citizens across the territory.

TAOA Director Paopao Kuresa then delivered remarks expressing gratitude to Governor Pulaalii, Congresswoman Amata, and the senior community for their presence and continued support. Director Paopao shared updates on TAOA initiatives, including efforts to revive travel programs for senior citizens in Tutuila and Manu‘a. He also announced that senior citizens will receive a pay increase to $7.25 beginning this July. The announcement was met with great satisfaction, filling the room with joy and appreciation.

Following the remarks, TAOA presented three distinguished awards:

● Oldest Senior Citizen Award, Awarded to Mrs. Pouiva Reid once again, age 101, the oldest TAOA‐registered senior citizen. The award was presented by Congresswoman Amata.

● Community Service Award, Presented to HTC Malepeai Setu, age 89, in recognition of his dedicated service to the community. The award was presented by Lions Club President Joey Cummings.

● Oldest SCSEP Employee Award, Awarded to Lolesio Faasau, age 76, honoring his continued service through the Senior Community Service Employment Program.

The program concluded with closing remarks by Deputy Director Faau Seumanutafa, followed by a closing prayer from Rev. Fetaiai Fui. A special dance and luncheon brought the celebration to a joyful close.

Section:

Local News

Tags:

Senior Citizens Day

https://www.samoanews.com/local-news/territorial-administration-aging-celebrates-senior-citizens-day

American Samoa STEAM Fest 2026 — Igniting Hope Through STEAM

(date: 2026-06-01)

Source: OceaniaCDN

Pago Pago, AMERICAN SAMOA — The American Samoa Community Development Network (OceaniaCDN), in partnership with community organizations, schools, government agencies, nonprofits, and local partners including the American Samoa Environmental Protection Agency (AS-EPA), held the American Samoa STEAM Fest 2026 this past Saturday.

This year’s theme, “Igniting Hope Through STEAM!”, highlighted the importance of Science, Technology, Engineering, Arts, and Mathematics (STEAM) in building pathways for innovation, education, workforce development, environmental stewardship, and community resilience throughout American Samoa.

STEAM Fest was a free, family-friendly community event designed to bring together students, educators, families, youth organizations, businesses, nonprofits, and government agencies to celebrate learning, creativity, and opportunity through interactive experiences and community collaboration.

The event featured:

● Interactive hands-on STEAM stations

● Art activities and live demonstrations

● Cultural and educational exhibits

● STEAM career exploration booths

● School clubs and student showcases

● Community resources and informational booths (Summer Camps for Youth)

● Family photo booth and local food vendors

● Prize giveaways and family activities (Gift Cards, SSY Bootcamp, Refrigerator, and more)

● Solar Car Competition and engineering challenges

One of the featured highlights of STEAM Fest 2026 was the continued Solar Car Challenge (formerly Junior Solar Sprint), where students applied engineering, teamwork, creativity, and problem-solving skills through solar-powered design competitions.

This year, OceaniaCDN was also piloting expanded participation opportunities for middle school and high school students, youth groups, and community-based teams to further increase access to engineering and technical education experiences for the youth of American Samoa.

In addition, STEAM Fest organizers invited student groups and schools to participate in a Student Expo Showcase featuring top projects and presentations from Science Fair competitions, HOSA events, Science Symposiums, and other academic and career technical education programs.

“As a community, we must continue creating opportunities that inspire our youth to explore innovation, leadership, culture, science, technology, and future career pathways,” organizers shared. “STEAM Fest is about bringing our community together to celebrate talent, creativity, and hope for the future of American Samoa.”

Local News

Obituaries for June 1

(date: 2026-06-01, updated: 2026-06-02)

Paulina Corrado Alvarado, 70, of Keaau died May 8, at Hale Anuenue Restorative Care Center in Hiloi. Born in Narvacan, Philippines, she was a flowers vendor at Hilo Farmers Market and member of St. Joseph Catholic Church. Visitation 4-6 p.m. Wednesday, June 3, at Dodo Mortuary Chapel. Wake service at 6 p.m. Visitation again 8-9:30 a.m. Thursday, June 4, at St. Joseph Catholic Church in Hilo. Funeral Mass at 9:30 a.m. Burial to follow at Homelani Memorial Park. Survived by husband, Eufemio Alvarado of Keaau; children, Ester Corrado (Juan) Garces, Alverita Corrado (Jose) Perez, Christopher (Daisy Ann) Alvarado, Davies (Ruthy) Alvarado and Mickey (Michelle) Alvarado of Keaau, Bernard (Cristy) Alvarado of Pepeekeo, Paul Alvarado of Keaau; 12 grandchildren; aunts, uncles, nieces and nephews. Arrangements by Dodo Mortuary.

U.S. Coast Guard Patrols Near Scarborough Shoal with Philippine Forces Amid Concerns of Potential Chinese Build-Up

(date: 2026-05-31, updated: 2026-06-10)

A U.S.-Philippine patrol near Scarborough Shoal this week amid Manila’s concerns of potential structures placed by Beijing at the South China Sea hotspot. The exercise from May 26 to 30 was the first time a U.S. Coast Guard vessel joined Philippine forces for a maritime cooperative activity. Also known as a joint patrol, the naval drills are designed to showcase the U.S.-Philippine alliance’s resolve in waters disputed by China. Washington and Manila committed to these drills in 2023, following a year of increasingly escalatory incidents from China at contested maritime features. Cutter USS Midgett (WMSL-757) joined the Philippine Navy frigate

Obituaries for May 31

(date: 2026-05-31, updated: 2026-06-01)

Mariano Carriaga Jr., 76, of Hilo died May 5 at Hilo Benioff Medical Center. Born in Hilo, he was a control operator for the former Hawaii Electric Light Co., state Golden Gloves boxing champion, drag racer and motocross champion. Visitation 10 a.m. to 5 p.m. Thursday, June 11 at the ARC of Hilo, 1099 Waianuenue Ave. Memorial service at 11:30 a.m. Casual attire; no flowers. Survived by wife, Darlene Carriaga of Hilo; sons, Troy (Michelle) Carriaga of Pearl City, Oahu, Mariano Carriaga III and Brandon (Sheri) Carriaga of Hilo; daughter, Erynn (James) Helenihi of Berthould, Colo.; brothers, Mark (Beverly) Carriaga of Kailua-Kona, Michael Carriaga Pascual of Ocean View, Carl “Littleman” (Pua) Carriaga of Keaau, Melvin Ray Carter of Napoopoo and Francis “Adobo” (Rochelle) Carter of Hilo; sisters, Marie (Dean) Rocha of Kailua-Kona and Monalisa (Gary Wana) Spalding of Hilo; stepmother, Ethel Carriaga of Hilo; 15 grandchildren and nine great-grandchildren; aunts, uncles, cousins, nieces and nephews. Arrangements by Dodo Mortuary.

The post Obituaries for May 31 appeared first on Hawaii Tribune-Herald.

Judge temporarily blocks Trump’s $1.8 billion ‘weaponization’ fund

(date: 2026-05-30, updated: 2026-05-31)

WASHINGTON — A U.S. judge on Friday temporarily blocked President Donald Trump’s administration from setting up a nearly $1.8 billion fund to compensate victims of what Trump has called government “weaponization.” The order by U.S. District Judge Leonie Brinkema of the Eastern District of Virginia blocks the Trump administration from “taking any further action” to set up or operate the fund while the judge hears additional legal arguments. The order will remain in effect at least until June 12. The Justice Department announced the creation of an “Anti-Weaponization Fund” last week as part of an agreement to settle Trump’s lawsuit against the Internal Revenue Service over the leak of his tax records.

The post Judge temporarily blocks Trump’s $1.8 billion ‘weaponization’ fund appeared first on Hawaii Tribune-Herald.

Judge won’t block Trump’s mail-in voting executive order for now

(date: 2026-05-29, updated: 2026-05-30)

A U.S. judge on Thursday declined to immediately block President Donald Trump’s executive order tightening rules on mail-in voting, but left the door open for the Democratic Party to challenge it again after the administration takes further steps to implement the measure. Washington-based U.S. District Judge Carl Nichols’ order did not address whether Trump’s March 31 order was lawful. It also does not, for now, change how Americans vote, since federal agencies have not yet enacted the new rules that the order called on them to adopt. Trump’s Republicans are in a tight battle to keep control of both houses of the U.S. Congress in the November midterm elections.

Jose of Hakalau; children, Jennifer (Reid) Shelton of Virginia, Michael (Stacy) Adachi of Hakalau and Alohilani Adachi-Jose of Hilo; many stepchildren; siblings, Denise (Bill) Harmon of North Highlands. (date: 2026-05-28, updated: 2026-06-09)

The Royal Navy’s mine countermeasures mothership RFA Lyme Bay (L3007) departed Tuesday from Gibraltar as the U.K. and France prepare to launch a multinational military mission to ensure freedom of navigation through the strait. More than 100 minehunting personnel from the Royal Navy’s diving and threat exploitation group and mine and threat exploitation group are on board Lyme Bay for a potential Strait of Hormuz mission, the Royal Navy announced. “RFA Lyme Bay’s potential deployment to the Gulf with advanced uncrewed minehunting systems is demonstrative of a shift towards agile, high-tech maritime capabilities. EPIC receives $25,000 donation from The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints

(date: 2026-05-28)

Pago Pago, AMERICAN SAMOA — EPIC (Empowering Pacific Island Communities) proudly announces the receipt of a generous $25,000 USD donation from The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints to assist with ongoing community outreach and support programs throughout American Samoa.

The donation was formally presented during a special visit by Sister Lesa Stevenson and Sister Anita Wakolo, whose presence and heartfelt support brought a spirit of gratitude, humility, and unity to the occasion.

During the presentation, Sister Stevenson expressed admiration for the broad impact EPIC is making throughout the territory.

“I really do not know when you sleep because you are covering so many aspects of life here and continually trying to make things better for those around you. As an educator myself, I especially appreciate what you are doing for young people — helping strengthen, protect, and empower them to become the best they can be.

“The work you are doing for families and communities is incredible. We are all God’s children, regardless of our different religious beliefs, and through your service to the people here, you are serving Him. For that, we are truly grateful.”

EPIC leadership expressed sincere appreciation for the Church’s generosity and continued commitment to humanitarian efforts that strengthen families and communities across the Pacific.

“This moment was both humbling and inspiring,” said Kapuana Ainu’u of EPIC. “There was a surreal feeling of gratitude as we reflected on the trust and confidence placed in our organization. This donation represents more than financial support — it represents hope, partnership, and a shared commitment to serving people in need.”

The funds will help strengthen several EPIC initiatives focused on youth development, emergency assistance, workforce readiness, wellness promotion, caregiver services, food security, and community outreach programs designed to uplift vulnerable individuals and families throughout the territory.

EPIC continues to work collaboratively with community organizations, educators, churches, and public agencies to provide meaningful support services and opportunities for self-reliance and empowerment.

The organization extends its deepest appreciation to The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints for its compassion, generosity, and dedication to humanitarian service.

For more information or to request services, please contact: Phone: (684) 688-EPIC (3742)

Website: www.asepic.org

Location: Leone, American Samoa (Across from the Leone Post Office)

Section:

Local News

Tags:

EPIC (Empowering Pacific Island Communities)

2 Killed in Eastern Pacific Strike on Suspected Narco Boat

(date: 2026-05-28, updated: 2026-06-05)

Two men were killed Wednesday in a strike on a suspected narco boat in the Eastern Pacific, U.S. Southern Command announced. The vessel, allegedly operated by an unidentified designated terrorist organization, is said to have been traveling along known narco-trafficking routes, USSOUTHCOM said. The strike was the 60th strike as part of Operation Southern Spear, the Trump administration’s campaign against drug cartels and maritime drug trafficking. North Korea Tests New Lightweight Launch System and Tactical Cruise Missiles

(date: 2026-05-27, updated: 2026-06-05)

North Korea test fired a newly-developed lightweight multi-purpose missile launching system and multiple tactical cruise missile weapons systems on Tuesday, state media Korean Central News Agency reported. North Korea’s Missile Administration and the Academy of Defense Science trialled the new missile launcher and missile systems as part of the country’s five-year plan to modernize its artillery and missile forces. The tests analyzed the power of the special mission tactical ballistic missile, the reliability of 240mm controlled artillery rockets with an ultra-precision autonomous navigation system that expanded firing range and the AI-guided hit accuracy of the tactical cruise missile, KCNA reported.

Local girl graduates Magna Cum Laude from Tufts University

(date: 2026-05-27)

She holds dual majors in Biology and Biotechnology

Boston, MASSACHUSETTS — Ann Mariel Recto Villanueva, daughter of Gemma and Ariel Villanueva, proudly graduates as Magna Cum Laude from Tufts University in Boston, Massachusetts, with dual majors in Bachelor of Science in Biology from School of Arts and Science and in Biotechnology from School of Engineering.

Born and raised in the village of Fagatogo, she was Co-Valedictorian of the Samoana High School Class of 2022. She went on to further her education at Tufts University (with an undergraduate acceptance rate of 9.7% in 2022), where she made history as the only student in her class from American Samoa.

Ann Mariel was a four-year recipient of the BLAST Cohort 11 Scholar and Knez Scholarship, which provided her with a full ride scholarship throughout her time at Tufts. On top of being a full-time student, she immersed herself in research and gained hands-on experience across a range of prestigious institutions.

In the summers of her college years, she served as a Clinical Trial Diversity Intern at Takeda Pharmaceutical (2023), a Research Assistant at Biohaven (2024), and a Puipui Malu Manatu Research Assistant I at American Samoa Community Cancer Coalition (2025). At Tufts, she worked as an Undergraduate Research Intern in Dr. Michael Levin’s Lab during her junior year and as an Undergraduate Volunteer Research Assistant in Dr. Basak Icli’s Lab at Tufts Medical Center during her senior year. Each of her research experiences fostered her interest in studying diseases disproportionately affecting Asian American, Native Hawaiian, and Pacific Islanders (AANHPIs) community.

Beyond the lab, she helped coordinate volunteer initiatives across the Greater Boston area through Tufts Project SHARE during her freshman year. From her second year of college onward, she served as Communication and Outreach Chair for the Tufts First Gen Collective, creating space and advocating for first generation students at Tufts University.

Her achievements are lovingly dedicated to her parents, Gemma and Ariel, whose sacrifices and unwavering love made every dream possible for their kids. Through long days behind the smoke of a barbeque grill, they gave everything so she could have everything. This accomplishment is much as it is hers. To her siblings, Clare, Ariel Jr., and Kenneth, for the constant love and cheer that carried her through.

Section:

Local News

Tags:

Ann Mariel Recto Villanueva

https://www.samoanews.com/local-news/local-girl-graduates-magna-cum-laude-tufts-university

Chinese Use Electronic Warfare Attacks on Dutch Warship in South China Sea, Says PLA

(date: 2026-05-27, updated: 2026-06-05)

Chinese naval and air forces drove off a Dutch air defense frigate near a disputed archipelago using warnings and electronic interference, Beijing’s South China Sea command said Wednesday. According to the People’s Liberation Army’s Southern Theater Command (PLA STC), the HNLMS De Ruyter (F804) and its embarked helicopter intruded Wednesday over the airspace of the Paracel Islands, an archipelago under the control of Beijing following its violent seizure from Vietnam in the 1970s. TSMC's employee bonuses expected to grow over 30% this year: Source

Local annual Arbor Week celebration holds first-ever Indoor Greenhouse Gala

(date: 2026-05-26)

By

Pauliana Angel Vitale

ASCC staff

Pago Pago, AMERICAN SAMOA — The annual Arbor Week celebration in American Samoa reached new heights this year with the first-ever Indoor Greenhouse Gala, held on May 1, 2026, at the ASCC Multi-Purpose Center. The evening brought together over 200 community members, leaders, government agencies, and students for a night dedicated to sustainability and environmental stewardship.

Hosted by the American Samoa Community College - Agriculture, Community and Natural Resources (ASCC-ACNR) division, the event was led by Extension Program Director Tialavea Molly Asifoa-Lagai and Territorial Forester Denis Sene, in partnership with the Manatua Nonprofit Organization under the leadership of Annie Amosa-Lei Sam and Lila Tuilagi.

Beyond the festivities, the Gala celebrated the significant progress made possible by recent investments in local forestry. In 2023, the ASCC-ACNR Forestry Program was awarded $750,000 through the USDA Forest Service Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) program. After a highly competitive grant cycle, American Samoa successfully secured these funds to directly benefit the territory. Through the Forestry Advisory Council, the grant was subsequently sub-awarded to four local nonprofit organizations: Manatua, Leasina 4-H, Finafinau, and the 4-H Youth Council.

The primary objective of this IRA grant is to support disadvantaged communities by providing technical and educational assistance for urban forestry projects. Since receiving the funding, these nonprofit partners have implemented numerous conservation and restoration initiatives involving youth groups across the islands.

To further support this engagement, the Forestry Program also hired five dedicated IRA interns: Lessie Faumuina, Eseta Enesi, Grace Nai, Grace Wanjau, and Lafaele Aunoa to serve as vital liaisons between the program and participating organizations.

Building on this foundation of collaboration, the Gala marked a milestone by recognizing the accomplishments of these partners while honoring the broader work of terrestrial-based environmental agencies.

For the evening, the ASCC Multi-Purpose Center was transformed into a lush, greenhouse-inspired venue that emphasized our local forests and environment. For many in attendance, the evening symbolized the growing momentum behind sustainability efforts throughout the territory.

Educational outreach remained central to the Gala, with a diverse range of agencies hosting informational booths — including the Department of Marine and Wildlife Resources, ASEPA, the Department of Agriculture, the Governor’s Resilience Office, the National Park of American Samoa, Fa’aSamoa Arts, and Tropical Trees and Seas.

The evening was further enriched by cultural performances from the 7th-grade Samoan class of Laulii Elementary, led by Principal Petesa Paselio and Ms. Tilofia Moetala.

A standout highlight was the sustainable fashion show presented by the 4-H Program. High school students from across the island — including Faasao Marist, Pacific Horizons, Tafuna, Nuuuli Voc-Tech, Fagaitua, and Leone — modeled creative designs crafted entirely from recycled and natural materials.

The Gala was honored by the presence of Father Pio Afu, who opened the evening with a word of prayer, setting a spirit of unity and shared purpose. He later brought the evening to a close with words of encouragement and a final prayer, reminding those in attendance that the environment is a gift from God, urging them to remain dedicated stewards of the Lord’s creations.

Adding to the prestige of the evening, Lieutenant Governor Pulumataala Ae Ae Jr. delivered special remarks on the importance of community-driven conservation. He expressed his enthusiastic support for the ongoing collaboration between the public and private sectors working to preserve the territory’s natural resources.

Support for the event was also evident from the Board of Higher Education, represented by Chairwoman Dr. Oreta Mapu Crichton and Secretary Pualani Pulu Fui, alongside ASCC President Dr. Rosevonne Pato and ACNR Director Aufa’i Apulu Ropeti Areta.

The success of the Gala was a collective effort, made possible by the support of the dedicated staff and leadership across ASCC, including Vice Presidents Dr. Moananu and Dr. Leomiti, CIO Grace Tulafono, Procurement Officer Jessie Su’esu’e, Finance Officer Elsie Lesā, Maintenance Manager Apolonia Aiolupotea, and Chief of Security Faamasani Tuitasi.

Following the event's success, organizers from ASCC-ACNR and Manatua expressed deep gratitude for the overwhelming community support and participation. They noted that the Gala’s success reflects a growing awareness of environmental stewardship and a stronger community commitment to protecting American Samoa’s natural resources.

As the first of its kind, the Indoor Greenhouse Gala has established a promising precedent for future Arbor Week celebrations, merging culture, youth engagement, and community action in a meaningful and impactful way.

Section:

Local News

Tags:

arbor week

https://www.samoanews.com/local-news/local-annual-arbor-week-celebration-holds-first-ever-indoor-greenhouse-gala

South Korea Announces Plan to Build Domestic Nuclear Attack Boat

(date: 2026-05-26, updated: 2026-06-03)

South Korea will build a new class of nuclear-powered attack boats to counter North Korea’s growing missile submarine threat, Seoul announced Tuesday. The nuclear submarines “will be developed and constructed entirely within the Republic of Korea,” reads a Ministry of National Defense document published Tuesday as part of the announcement that details the Republic of Korea’s Nuclear-powered Submarine Initiative. The formal announcement of a homegrown nuclear attack boat follows last fall’s approval from Washington to support South Korea’s desire for nuclear fuel. South Korea needs U.S. support to acquire fuel for the boats based on the 2015 Atomic Energy Peaceful

Obituaries for May 26

(date: 2026-05-26, updated: 2026-05-27)

Sheila Santos Makuakane Erece, 77, of Hilo, formerly of Papaikou, died April 7 at home. Born in Hilo, she was homemaker and former secretary for Immaculate Heart of Mary Catholic Church in Papaikou. Visitation 9-10 a.m. Saturday, June 6, at Immaculate Heart of Mary Catholic Church in Papaikou. Memorial Mass at 10 a.m. Urn committal and burial to follow at Mauna Kea Memorial Park. Survived by husband, Glen R. Erece of Hilo; brother, Robert Santos of Pahoa; daughters, Marley (Teody Raguindin) Green, Marla (Jayson) Makuakane-Castillas, Larena (Sherman Sr.) Richards; Glenneen (Francois Hocson) Erece; mother in-law, Maria Erece of Papaikou; brothers- and sisters-in-law; grandchildren and great-grandchildren. Arrangements by Dodo Mortuary.

Obituaries for May 24

(date: 2026-05-24, updated: 2026-05-25)

Remedios Yanos Ebanez, 86, of Hilo, formerly of Washington, died Nov. 19 at a foster family home in Hilo. Born in Kohala, she was a retired accountant for the U.S. Department of Defense. Visitation 8:30-9:30 a.m. Saturday, June 13, at Sacred Heart Catholic Church in Hawi. Funeral Mass at 10 a.m. Urn committal service to follow at the church cemetery. Survived by sons, Damien Ebanez of Florida and Guy Ebanez of Hawi; daughters, Rhoda Usman of Washington and Jeanette (Ben) Castillo of Pahala; daughter-in-law, Kristi (Ted) Ebanez of Oahu; brother, Ronald Yanos of Oahu; sister, Joy Lorita of California; nine grandchildren and 12 great-grandchildren; cousins, nieces and nephews. Arrangements by Dodo Mortuary.
